name: Connecticut
tagline: A $55.8B two-year budget, a state CIO consolidating IT under DAS, and a governor who blocked sweeping AI rules in 2025 (a narrower AI law passed in 2026) and is throttling data centers over energy costs — Connecticut is a centralized, cost-conscious market that rewards governed modernization over hype.

execSummary.summary: Connecticut budgets on a two-year cycle; the FY2026–27 biennial budget signed in June 2025 totals roughly $55.8B (~$27.1B in FY2026, ~$28.4B in FY2027), and a FY2027 adjustment (Public Act 26-68) was signed in May 2026. For a Dell seller, three things shape the motion. First, technology is centralized: State CIO Mark Raymond runs IT for ~39 executive-branch agencies through the Department of Administrative Services' Bureau of Enterprise Systems & Technology (BEST), and IT buys above $500K route through BEST. Second, the policy posture is restraint, not acceleration — Gov. Lamont's veto threat killed the sweeping SB 2 AI bill in 2025 (a narrower AI law, Public Act 26-15, was enacted in May 2026), and he is publicly telling new data centers to 'slow down' unless they bring their own power generation, because Connecticut's wholesale energy costs run far above the national average. Third, the real demand is unglamorous but durable: IT consolidation and shared services, a 'whole-of-state' cybersecurity push funded partly by federal grants, governed AI pilots inside agencies, and the K-12/library/municipal network (Connecticut Education Network) plus the UConn/Yale research base. Sell governed modernization and lifecycle value, not speculative AI-factory scale.
opportunities: IT consolidation and shared services under BEST — server, storage, and infrastructure refresh as the state standardizes across ~39 agencies.
'Whole-of-state' cybersecurity for state agencies and municipalities, backed by federal State & Local Cybersecurity Grant funding and 24/7 monitoring already in place.
Governed, on-prem / private-cloud AI pilots inside agencies and the Commission for Educational Technology — a fit given the state's caution about open public models.
Higher-ed and research computing at UConn and Yale, plus the Connecticut Education Network serving K-12, libraries, and most municipalities.

stats.label: State AI law (2026)
stats.note: Narrower AI law enacted May 2026 after the sweeping SB 2 failed in 2025

budget.intro: Connecticut appropriates on a two-year (biennial) cycle. The FY2026–27 budget signed in June 2025 totals roughly $55.8B — about $27.1B in FY2026 and $28.4B in FY2027 — and stays under the state's spending cap. There is no single published 'state IT budget' line; technology spend is distributed across agencies and centrally coordinated by DAS/BEST, with a FY2027 mid-biennium adjustment (Public Act 26-68) signed in May 2026.
notes: Budget figures are biennial; technology spend is not a single line item — it is distributed across agencies and coordinated centrally by DAS/BEST.
Connecticut operates under a statutory spending cap; the posture is cost-conscious, so frame value as consolidation, lifecycle, and avoided cost rather than net-new spend.

FY2027 (governor's proposed adjustment, per NASBO): $25.5B general fund, +0.4% vs enacted FY2027 (+4.5% vs FY2026); $28.7B all funds. A mid-biennium adjustment that holds a $5.8B budget reserve and stays under the spending cap.

initiatives.name: IT Consolidation & Shared Services (DAS/BEST)
initiatives.summary: State CIO Mark Raymond is centralizing technology for roughly 39 executive-branch agencies under DAS and the Bureau of Enterprise Systems & Technology (BEST), emphasizing shared solutions across agencies and leveraging combined purchasing power. This is the most reliable, funded motion in the state — infrastructure refresh, storage, and managed services that map to standardization rather than agency-by-agency sprawl.

initiatives.name: Whole-of-State Cybersecurity
initiatives.summary: Connecticut is pursuing federal grants (State & Local Cybersecurity Grant Program) to extend a 'whole-of-state' strategy to municipalities, with 24/7 security monitoring and advanced endpoint protection already deployed to state agencies. Federal grant timing is uncertain, but the direction — secure infrastructure, endpoint, and managed security spanning state and local — is durable demand.

initiatives.name: Governed AI Adoption (narrow 2026 AI law)
initiatives.summary: After the sweeping SB 2 AI-regulation bill passed the Senate 32-4 in May 2025 but died in the House under a Lamont veto threat, Connecticut enacted a narrower AI law (Public Act 26-15) in May 2026. Agencies are piloting AI cautiously (including in education via the CIO-chaired Commission for Educational Technology). The opening is governed, auditable, private/on-prem AI for sensitive data, not open public models.

initiatives.name: Data Centers — Throttled by Energy Costs
initiatives.summary: Unlike Sun Belt states, Connecticut is deliberately slowing hyperscale data centers: a 1.5M-sq-ft campus tied to the Millstone nuclear plant was scrubbed, and Lamont's 2026 State of the State urged new data centers to add their own generation. Wholesale energy costs run far above the U.S. average, and a 20–30 year tax-incentive program has drawn only one taker. Do not lead with AI-factory scale here — the in-state opportunity is governed enterprise AI and research computing, not hyperscale buildout.

procurement.intro: The Department of Administrative Services (DAS) is the central procurement authority, and its Bureau of Enterprise Systems & Technology (BEST) is the central technology organization. Most state technology buys run through DAS statewide (master) contracts via the CTsource eProcurement portal; IT procurements above $500,000 are reviewed by BEST. DAS can also approve agency use of certain federal vehicles (e.g., GSA) under cooperative-contracting authority.

vehicles.name: DAS Statewide / Master Contracts
vehicles.detail: Primary vehicle. Pre-competed agreements for hardware, software, and services available to state agencies and many municipalities — confirm Dell agreements and resellers are current and correctly scoped.

vehicles.name: CTsource eProcurement Portal
vehicles.detail: The single statewide portal to register, search, and respond to solicitations and contracts; register and monitor here.

vehicles.name: Cooperative / GSA contracts
vehicles.detail: DAS can approve agency use of certain federal contracts (e.g., GSA) under CGS 4a-66(b) — a path where a statewide contract is absent.

entities.name: DAS / BEST
entities.role: Central procurement and central IT; reviews IT buys above $500K and sets statewide technology direction.

entities.name: Dept. of Emergency Services & Public Protection (DESPP)
entities.role: Public-safety technology buyer; State Police modernization funded in FY2027.

entities.name: UConn
entities.role: Large research-computing and edtech buyer; a DAS–UConn purchasing partnership saves the state ~$38M annually.

entities.name: Yale University
entities.role: Major private research institution and independent research-computing / AI-infrastructure buyer.

procurement.competition: Connecticut is a centralized, cost-disciplined market. Buying runs through DAS statewide contracts and the BEST review gate, and the state operates under a spending cap — so differentiation comes from consolidation/shared-services fit, governed-AI and security capability, and lifecycle/avoided-cost value rather than raw price or speculative AI scale.

powerStructure.name: Ned Lamont
powerStructure.title: Governor
powerStructure.note: Cost-conscious; blocked the broad SB 2 AI bill in 2025 (a narrower AI law passed in 2026) and is throttling new data centers over energy costs. Favors targeted tech (quantum in Fairfield County, an AI center near Stamford).

powerStructure.name: Mark Raymond
powerStructure.title: State Chief Information Officer
powerStructure.org: DAS / Bureau of Enterprise Systems & Technology (BEST)
powerStructure.note: On the state leadership team since 2011; runs IT for ~39 executive agencies, the Connecticut Education Network, and chairs the Commission for Educational Technology. Owns the statewide tech direction.

powerStructure.name: Sen. James Maroney
powerStructure.title: Lead AI legislator
powerStructure.note: Lead author of Connecticut's AI legislation (SB 2 in 2025; the enacted 2026 law); a national voice on state AI policy. Useful for reading where AI governance — and agency AI demand — heads next.

powerStructure.name: Office of Policy and Management (OPM)
powerStructure.title: Budget & IT strategic planning
powerStructure.note: Publishes the state IT strategic plan and the biennial budget — the source of truth for forecasting tech priorities and spend.

news.title: Lamont signs FY2027 budget adjustment (Public Act 26-68)
news.impact: Mid-biennium adjustment includes $9.86M for State Police technology modernization; confirms a steady, cap-bound spending posture.

news.title: Gov. Lamont proposed a $25.5B general-fund FY2027 budget adjustment
news.impact: $28.7B all funds, +0.4% GF vs enacted FY2027; a mid-biennium adjustment holding a $5.8B reserve under the spending cap.

news.title: Lamont's State of the State urges data centers to 'slow down'
news.impact: Signals Connecticut will not chase hyperscale AI buildout — frame in-state AI as governed enterprise/research workloads, not data-center scale.

news.title: CIO Mark Raymond profiled on IT consolidation and AI in education
news.impact: Reinforces shared-services strategy and cautious, governed AI adoption through the Commission for Educational Technology.

news.title: Lamont signs ~$55.8B FY2026–27 biennial budget
news.impact: Two-year spending plan under the state cap sets the funding baseline for agency technology priorities.

news.title: Sweeping SB 2 AI bill passes Senate 32-4 but dies in the House
news.impact: No broad AI statute in 2025; a narrower AI law (Public Act 26-15) followed in May 2026 — agencies proceed with governed, auditable AI.

news.title: CT weighs more AI data centers; energy costs remain the obstacle
news.impact: Highlights why hyperscale demand is muted in-state — wholesale energy costs run far above the U.S. average.

dellActions: Lead with consolidation and shared services: tie Dell infrastructure, storage, and lifecycle offerings to BEST's standardization across ~39 agencies, and frame value as avoided cost under the spending cap.
Engage BEST early on any IT pursuit above $500K — that threshold triggers central review, so align stakeholders and statewide-contract coverage before the deal matures.
Position 'whole-of-state' security: secure infrastructure, endpoint, and managed services that extend from state agencies to municipalities, mapped to federal cybersecurity grant cycles.
Sell governed, private/on-prem AI for sensitive workloads — a fit for a state that enacted only narrow AI regulation (2026) and stays cautious about open public models and hyperscale data centers.
Cultivate UConn and Yale as research-computing and AI-infrastructure accounts, and leverage the existing DAS–UConn purchasing partnership and the Connecticut Education Network footprint.
